does: This paper introduces MM-CreativityBench, a benchmark designed to evaluate whether large multimodal models can discover creative, physically feasible solutions to open-ended problems in visual scenes. Rather than testing pattern recognition or question answering, the benchmark requires models to identify how objects in a scene can be repurposed in non-obvious ways—a form of creative tool use grounded in visual affordances and physical constraints. The benchmark provides scenario images with structured views of candidate entities and their parts, enabling fine-grained, interactive evaluation of how models inspect scenes, identify affordances, and compose solutions. The authors conduct experiments on current LMMs and find that these models fall short despite possessing generative capabilities, suggesting fundamental gaps in their ability to reason about creative physical problem-solving.
connects: This work builds on the recent rapid advancement of large multimodal models in perception and reasoning tasks, extending the evaluation of these systems beyond standard benchmarks that focus on well-posed visual question answering and pattern recognition. It connects to longstanding research on affordance perception and tool use in cognitive science and robotics, as well as prior work on evaluating reasoning capabilities in visual domains. The paper responds to a gap in current ML benchmarks that test narrow task performance but overlook creative problem-solving—a capability central to human intelligence. It builds on existing work in visually grounded reasoning and physical reasoning by introducing a more demanding evaluation that requires iterative inspection and compositional thinking about object repurposing.
enables: This benchmark enables future work to develop and evaluate LMMs that can engage in genuinely creative, physically grounded reasoning rather than pattern matching. It opens directions for improving how models learn to identify and compose affordances in visually rich environments, potentially informing better architectures for embodied AI and robotic systems that must solve novel problems with available tools. The interactive evaluation framework could drive research into multi-step reasoning and visual inspection strategies in multimodal models. The work also points toward developing better training methods and datasets that emphasize creative problem-solving, potentially advancing capabilities needed for robots and AI systems operating in open-ended real-world environments where solutions are non-obvious and require physical feasibility reasoning.
